hook: You already know how to find areas and volumes using integrals — powerful tools that changed how we measure shapes. But the real world also asks questions like: how much cable does a suspension bridge need, how hard does water push against a dam, and where does an irregularly shaped object balance? This unit reveals that integration is not just a geometric tool but a universal language for measuring the physical world.
concept: The unifying idea is that any quantity that accumulates continuously over a curve, surface, or region can be captured by an integral — formally expressed through the principle of *Riemann summation applied to physical and geometric measures*.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to compute the exact length of a curved path by summing infinitely many tiny straight-line segments using the formula $L = \int_a^b \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2}\, dx$.
Master the technique of finding the surface area generated when a curve is rotated about an axis, extending arc length into three-dimensional surfaces.
Apply integration to calculate the work done by a variable force, the work needed to pump fluids, and the hydrostatic pressure exerted on submerged surfaces.
You will use weighted integrals — called moments — to locate the exact balance point of a lamina or system with continuously distributed mass.
Apply definite integrals to continuous probability distributions, computing probabilities, means, and variances for real-world random variables using probability density functions.
